content.assessment: This proposal's rationale is strongly reinforced by the lens evidence's own meta-level finding: the systematic review explicitly notes that almost all rural depopulation policy literature is European and focused on quantitative outcome variables, with no study addressing qualitative aspects, and cautions against assuming international transferability (F-live-international_comparison-10). This directly supports the proposal's core premise that Hungary needs its own outcome tracking rather than relying on imported evidence. The proposal's design also implicitly responds to the contested and context-dependent nature of closure effects seen across Finland, Saxony, Sweden, and Denmark (F-live-international_comparison-01), and to divergent national mechanisms (Scotland, France, Wales, Austria) that have not been validated against each other, reinforcing the case for an evidence-generation layer rather than direct policy transplant.
opportunities: A national registry could systematically test which international findings (e.g., Finnish/Saxon depopulation-acceleration mechanism, Swedish political-cost effect) actually hold in the Hungarian context, directly operationalizing the lens's core caution about cross-country causal transfer

Registry design could incorporate Scotland's documentation requirements (educational benefit, community effect, alternatives assessed) as a data standard even without adopting the full presumption mechanism (F-live-international_comparison-02)
strengths: Directly aligned with the lens's own limitation statement: the systematic review finding that policy transferability evidence is European-only and purely quantitative, with no qualitative validation, supports building a Hungary-specific evidence base rather than assuming transfer (F-live-international_comparison-10)

The proposal's demand to track realized outcomes rather than assumed international patterns is consistent with the genuinely mixed cross-country findings on whether closure causes depopulation (Finland/Denmark find a link; Saxony/Sweden do not) (F-live-international_comparison-01)
threats: Historical pattern of top-down political decisions overriding technical evidence, as seen destructively in Poland's 2017 reversal (referenced outside this lens) and generally in Hungary's own cyclical policy history, suggests an information-only framework may not change outcomes absent binding authority

Diversity of international approaches (Scotland presumption, Wales guidance, France contracts, Austria reconciliation) with no clear convergence suggests even a well-designed registry may struggle to identify a single transferable 'best practice' benchmark
weaknesses: An evidence-and-transparency framework alone does not resolve the institutional precondition problems (loss of municipal bargaining power under KLIK) that constrain transfer of substantive mechanisms like Scotland's or France's (F-live-international_comparison-04)
